question: Does your firm have a written AI usage policy that all attorneys have signed?
proof_snippet: Johnson v. Dunn — Three Butler Snow attorneys disqualified. Court found firm had no written AI policy despite using AI tools regularly.
why: Courts are treating the absence of a written policy as evidence of systemic negligence. In Johnson, the lack of policy turned an individual error into firm-wide liability.
remediation: Draft and distribute a firm-wide AI use policy. Require all attorneys to sign an acknowledgment. Review and update quarterly as court rules evolve.

question: Do you independently verify every citation in AI-assisted drafts against primary sources?
proof_snippet: Couvrette v. Wisnovsky — $109,700 sanctions. 23 fabricated citations and 8 false quotations across three filings. Largest AI sanction in U.S. history.
why: Every single AI sanctions case involves unverified citations. Courts view failure to verify as a violation of Rule 11 obligations. This is the #1 risk factor.
remediation: Require a second attorney to independently verify every AI-generated citation against Westlaw, CourtListener, or Google Scholar before filing. No self-verification.

question: Do you verify output from paid legal AI tools (CoCounsel, Lexis+AI) the same way you verify free tools?
proof_snippet: United States v. Farris — CJA fees denied, attorney removed. Brief filename read 'CoCounsel Skill Results.' Paid tool hallucinated. Court: 'The tool's pedigree is no defense.'
why: Courts have now sanctioned attorneys for trusting paid tools like CoCounsel (Westlaw). K&L Gates was sanctioned in Lacey despite using CoCounsel. Paying for AI does not equal verification.
remediation: Apply the same verification protocol to ALL AI tools — free and paid. CoCounsel, Lexis+AI, and CaseText can and do hallucinate. Document the verification step.

question: Have all attorneys at your firm completed AI competency training in the past 12 months?
proof_snippet: Montes v. Suns — Court ordered mandatory AI ethics training as part of sanctions. Judge forwarded ruling to every federal judge in Arizona.
why: ABA Formal Opinion 512 requires AI competency. Courts are now ordering training as sanctions — meaning they expect it to already be in place. Untrained attorneys are walking liabilities.
remediation: Implement mandatory annual AI ethics CLE training. Cover: hallucination risks, verification protocols, disclosure requirements, and ethical obligations under ABA Opinion 512.

question: Does a senior attorney review all AI-assisted work product before filing?
proof_snippet: Johnson v. Dunn — Practice group leader's failure to review called 'particularly egregious.' Three attorneys disqualified, firm referred to Alabama State Bar.
why: Supervisory liability is expanding. In Lacey, two BigLaw firms were sanctioned because the supervising attorney reviewed for style but not substance. Courts expect substantive review.
remediation: Designate a partner or AI governance committee to review all AI-assisted filings before submission. Create a sign-off checklist. Every signer is personally liable.

question: Do you have a protocol for AI disclosure when required by local court rules?
proof_snippet: Prososki v. Regan — Attorney denied AI use despite 57 defective citations out of 63. Brief stricken. Referred for discipline and facing suspension.
why: 300+ courts now have AI disclosure rules. Denying AI use when evidence suggests otherwise is treated as an aggravating factor. Prososki's denial turned sanctions into potential disbarment.
remediation: Maintain a current list of AI disclosure requirements for every jurisdiction where your firm practices. Build disclosure language into your filing templates.

question: Do your engagement letters address AI tool usage in client matters?
proof_snippet: Johnson v. Dunn — Firm disqualified from representing client. Client's case damaged by undisclosed AI use. No engagement letter provisions for AI.
why: When AI errors damage a client's case, the first question in malpractice litigation will be: did the client consent to AI use? Engagement letters are your first line of defense.
remediation: Add an AI use clause to all engagement letters specifying: which tools may be used, how client data is protected, and that AI output is always human-verified before delivery.

question: Does your firm maintain an approved list of AI tools with usage guidelines for each?
proof_snippet: Lacey v. State Farm — K&L Gates sanctioned $31,100. Attorney used both CoCounsel AND Google Gemini without firm guidelines on either tool.
why: Shadow AI is the biggest governance risk. When attorneys use unapproved tools, the firm has no visibility and no defense. An approved tools list creates accountability.
remediation: Create and maintain an approved AI tools list. Specify permitted use cases for each tool. Require approval before any new tool is used on client matters. Block consumer-grade tools on firm networks.

question: Do you maintain audit trails showing which parts of a filing were AI-assisted?
proof_snippet: United States v. Farris — Court caught AI use from the filename 'CoCounsel Skill Results.' Attorney had no documentation of what was AI-generated vs. human-written.
why: When sanctions hit, courts examine the entire drafting process. An audit trail proves good faith. Without one, every filing becomes suspect — and sanctions can cascade across cases.
remediation: Implement a tagging system in your document workflow: mark AI-drafted sections, log which tool was used, and record the verification step. This creates a defensible record.

question: Does your firm have a documented response plan for when AI errors are discovered in filed documents?
proof_snippet: Renfer/Fivehouse — AUSA panicked after losing a draft, had AI rewrite it, then tried to cover it up. Fired by DOJ. No incident response plan existed.
why: Courts treat cover-ups far more severely than errors. A documented response plan — immediate disclosure, correction filing, client notification — can turn a sanction into a warning.
remediation: Create a three-step protocol: (1) Immediately disclose to the court via corrective filing. (2) Notify the client with a candid explanation. (3) Document the root cause and update internal protocols.
